notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - Agent as "digital human": an agent has a "brain" (state, prompts, model) and can use "tools" (external utilities, apps).
  - Tools: any external utility used to accomplish work (laptop, internet, calculator, phone apps). Agents may or may not need tools for a task.
  - Base agent class: present in autogen core; can be extended/implemented to create custom agents (but pre-built agents cover most needs).
  - Preset / pre-built agents (provided by autogen agent chat high-level API): ready-made agent types to use without subclassing.
  - Agent attributes shared across agents:
    - name: identifier for an agent (no spaces allowed as of version 6.1).
    - description: a one-line textual ID describing the agent; NOT used to control agent behavior.
    - system prompt (separate): the argument that controls how the agent behaves.
  - Agent methods:
    - run: runs the agent on a string or list of messages; returns a task result. Intended to be called with new messages (not full history). Agents are stateful and remember past state.
    - run_stream: same goal as run but returns an iterator/stream of intermediate messages (subclass of base chat message) followed by a task result — used to get updates as work proceeds.
  - Stateful agents: agents maintain internal state (preferences, prior context) so callers should send only new messages rather than the full conversation history.
  - Autogen core API: agent is an interface in the core API to implement for custom behavior.
  - Practical coverage claim: pre-built agents cover ~95–99% of use cases.

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - "Just like we are human" — agent compared to a human with a brain and tools.
  - Tools = things we use in daily life (laptop, internet, calculator, phone apps).
  - Coffee example to illustrate run vs run_stream:
    - run = go do the whole task (make/purchase coffee) then come back with result.
    - run_stream = provide step-by-step updates (I'm 45 minutes going; I have reached the shop; I have paid; here's what I ordered).

- Any code, commands, or config shown
  - Mentions of imports (no exact code shown, but referenced):
    - importing the assistant agent (the "agent" which is my assistant agent).
    - importing an "open AI chat condition client from model" (transcript phrasing; likely an OpenAI chat client).
    - importing messages (used to connect with agents).
  - Selecting a kernel in the environment before running code (mentioned: "let me select the kernel here").

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Agent name cannot contain spaces as of autogen version 6.1 (may change in future).
  - Agent description does NOT influence agent behavior — use system prompt to control behavior.
  - run should be called with new messages only, not the complete history, since agents are stateful and will remember prior state.
  - run_stream returns intermediate messages (stream) while run returns the completed summary/result.
  - If you need custom behavior you must implement/extend the base agent interface in the autogen core; but usually pre-built agents suffice.

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - run vs run_stream:
    - run: execute task, return final result / overall summary.
    - run_stream: execute task, yield intermediate messages/updates, then final task result.
  - Agent kinds / pre-built agents mentioned:
    - assistant agent: assists with tasks; can have tools, model context, system message.
    - code executor agent: executes code (may or may not have a "mind").
    - user proxy agent: behaves as a user; useful for human-in-the-loop scenarios.
    - society-of-mind agent: uses an inner team of agents to generate responses (recently added).
    - message filter config agent (referred to as "message filter conf uh agent").
    - base chat agent.
    - base agent (core interface) — implementable in autogen core.
  - Agent components/options applicable to many agents:
    - tools (multiple)
    - model/context
    - system message / system prompt
    - run / run_stream methods
  - Use-case coverage:
    - Pre-built agents are said to cover ~95% (then corrected to ~99%) of use cases.

- Direct quotes worth preserving
  - "description is not something which agent takes into play when it is doing his job."
  - "you cannot have spaces in between names as of version 6.1"
  - "Agent are expected to be stateful and this method is expected to be called with new messages not complete history."
  - "Agents are stateful by default in autogen and they will remember that."
